content.claim: Poland's 2017 reversal, which re-extended primary education to 8 years and abolished the gimnazjum middle tier, produced severe implementation costs including school closures (approximately 7,000 junior high schools), potential job losses (union estimate of 10,000 teachers), double-cohort overcrowding in high schools, and widespread teacher strikes, regardless of the reform's substantive merits.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Polish system reform lens
limitations: Implementation disruption is distinct from structural efficacy; transition costs do not determine whether the original 6+3+3 or reverted 8+4 structure is preferable
Teacher union estimates of job losses (10,000) are not independently verified in sourced materials
Overcrowding and late-evening lessons are documented for the transition period but longer-run effects are unclear
Political process (lack of consultation, rapid parliamentary passage) is documented but does not resolve whether stakeholder input would have changed the structural decision
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
